does: This paper presents SeedHijack, a supply-chain attack targeting the pseudorandom number generators used in LLM sampling during autoregressive generation. By hijacking PRNG seed values, the attack forces models to select specific tokens without modifying underlying model logits, demonstrating 99.6% success on GPT-2 and 100% success on aligned models up to 7B parameters across multiple alignment approaches. The authors benchmark the attack across nine sampling configurations and show it bypasses existing alignment defenses. As a defense, they propose integrating hardware-based quantum random number generators into the sampling pipeline, which neutralizes the attack with minimal computational overhead of 0.6% latency increase and 7.7 MB additional memory per their evaluation.
connects: This work extends research on LLM vulnerabilities and backdoor attacks by identifying an underexplored attack surface at the sampling layer rather than in model weights or training procedures. It builds on prior work examining supply-chain attacks and PRNG vulnerabilities in cryptographic systems, adapting those insights to the generative AI context. The paper responds to growing literature on adversarial robustness and alignment of large language models, showing that defenses focusing on logits and training procedures fail to protect against sampling-layer manipulation. It connects to broader concerns about trustworthiness in deployed ML systems and the importance of securing infrastructure components beyond the neural network itself.
enables: This research opens several future directions for both attack and defense research in LLM systems. It motivates deeper investigation of sampling-layer vulnerabilities and other infrastructure-level attack surfaces in generative models that current security frameworks overlook. The QRNG defense approach establishes a template for hardware-based security interventions in ML systems, potentially extending to other components relying on randomness. The work enables more comprehensive threat modeling that accounts for supply-chain scenarios and encourages the development of sampling algorithms inherently resistant to seed manipulation. It also points toward broader questions about securing production LLM deployments and the trade-offs between performance and security guarantees in real-world applications.
